---
title: "The tier list"
subtitle: "Journals with representative papers"
---

*A single-page catalogue of the main journals, grouped by tier. Each entry follows the same four-field schema (What it is / Focus / When to target / Representative papers), with links out to each journal's homepage and to cited papers via DOI. Verify citation details before quoting them elsewhere.*

::: {.callout-tip collapse="false"}
## Jump to a tier

- [S tier — field-defining flagships](#s-tier)
- [A tier — core homes for semiparametric and causal work](#a-tier)
- [B tier — strong and relevant, often audience-specific](#b-tier)
- [C tier — respectable niche and methods-applied outlets](#c-tier)
:::

---

::: {.tier-s}

# S tier — field-defining flagships {#s-tier}

The "Big Four" of statistics. A publication in any of them is a career event; multiple publications mark you as a serious methodologist. Review processes are long, reviewers are expert, and theorems are scrutinized.

## [Annals of Statistics](https://imstat.org/journals-and-publications/annals-of-statistics/) {#annals-of-statistics}

**What it is:** The theoretical flagship of the Institute of Mathematical Statistics. The most mathematically demanding venue in the field.

**Focus:** Rigorous theoretical statistics — minimax theory, empirical process theory, high-dimensional statistics, nonparametrics, asymptotic theory, semiparametric efficiency bounds.

**When to target:** Editorial signal is *"prove something sharp and new"* — papers without a main theorem are rarely competitive. Target AoS when your primary contribution is a theorem with clean statement and proof; if the contribution is methodological with lighter theory, JRSS-B or JASA T&M is a better fit.

**Representative papers:**

- **[Candès & Tao (2007), *The Dantzig selector: statistical estimation when p is much larger than n*](https://doi.org/10.1214/009053606000001523).** A cornerstone of high-dimensional regression theory; shows that a linear-programming-based estimator recovers sparse signals at near-oracle rates. Still the template for how sparsity/risk theorems are written.
- **[Bickel, Ritov & Tsybakov (2009), *Simultaneous analysis of Lasso and Dantzig selector*](https://doi.org/10.1214/08-AOS620).** Unified oracle inequalities for the two estimators under restricted eigenvalue conditions. A standard reference for anyone proving Lasso-type guarantees.
- **[Athey, Tibshirani & Wager (2019), *Generalized random forests*](https://doi.org/10.1214/18-AOS1709).** Asymptotic theory for honest forests estimating heterogeneous treatment effects and other local moment parameters — the paper that made causal forests rigorously inferential.

## [Journal of the American Statistical Association — Theory & Methods](https://www.tandfonline.com/journals/uasa20) {#jasa-tm}

**What it is:** The flagship general-interest statistics journal. T&M is the theoretical/methodological section (distinguished from Applications & Case Studies).

**Focus:** Broader than AoS — welcomes both deeply theoretical work and rigorous methodology with strong applied motivation. More willing than AoS to publish papers whose main contribution is a new method rather than a new theorem.

**When to target:** Editorial signal is *"a new result that matters, with proof."* JASA T&M is the natural home when the paper's contribution is a methodological advance supported by theory rather than a pure theorem; if the driving contribution is a substantive application, aim for JASA A&CS instead.

**Representative papers:**

- **[Robins, Rotnitzky & Zhao (1994), *Estimation of regression coefficients when some regressors are not always observed*](https://doi.org/10.1080/01621459.1994.10476818).** The seminal augmented IPW paper; introduces doubly-robust estimating equations that became the foundation of modern missing-data and causal inference.
- **[Tan (2006), *A distributional approach for causal inference using propensity scores*](https://doi.org/10.1198/016214506000000023).** Likelihood-based framing of doubly-robust estimation via the propensity score; influential in shaping the efficiency-bound perspective used in TMLE and DML.
- **[Wager & Athey (2018), *Estimation and inference of heterogeneous treatment effects using random forests*](https://doi.org/10.1080/01621459.2017.1319839).** Gave the first asymptotic normality results for forest-based conditional average treatment effect estimators — a bridge between ML and classical inference.

## [Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B (Statistical Methodology)](https://academic.oup.com/jrsssb) {#jrss-b}

**What it is:** The British analog to JASA T&M, with a slightly more methodological (as opposed to purely theoretical) bent. Historically *the* journal for seminal methodological papers.

**Focus:** High-quality methodology with theoretical grounding. Discussion papers are a unique feature — the editors invite prominent statisticians to comment on selected papers, and the authors respond. A discussion-paper invitation is a significant honor.

**When to target:** Editorial signal is *"methodology that will be cited for decades."* JRSS-B is ideal for a methodological advance you expect to become canonical, especially when you want the discussion-paper format; for shorter, tighter methodological pieces, Biometrika often fits better.

**Representative papers:**

- **[Tibshirani (1996), *Regression shrinkage and selection via the lasso*](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.2517-6161.1996.tb02080.x).** The paper that introduced the lasso — arguably the single most-cited statistics paper of the modern era, and the prototype for ℓ₁-regularization across statistics and ML.
- **[Benjamini & Hochberg (1995), *Controlling the false discovery rate: a practical and powerful approach to multiple testing*](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.2517-6161.1995.tb02031.x).** Redefined multiple-testing in the genomics era; the BH procedure is now baseline in any high-dimensional hypothesis-testing pipeline.
- **[Peters, Bühlmann & Meinshausen (2016), *Causal inference by using invariant prediction: identification and confidence intervals*](https://doi.org/10.1111/rssb.12167).** Introduced invariant causal prediction — identifying causal predictors as those whose conditional distribution is invariant across environments.

## [Biometrika](https://academic.oup.com/biomet) {#biometrika}

**What it is:** The oldest of the Big Four (founded 1901 by Karl Pearson, Galton and Weldon). Historically oriented toward biology and medicine but evolved into a broad-methodology venue.

**Focus:** Elegant methodology, often at the intersection of theory and application. Values clarity, concision, and mathematical polish. Known for shorter papers than AoS or JRSS-B.

**When to target:** Editorial signal is *"a beautiful result, beautifully presented."* Biometrika rewards polished, concise methodology with a clean mathematical core; if your paper needs extensive development or discussion, JRSS-B's longer format is the better venue.

**Representative papers:**

- **[Rosenbaum & Rubin (1983), *The central role of the propensity score in observational studies for causal effects*](https://doi.org/10.1093/biomet/70.1.41).** The paper that made the propensity score the lingua franca of observational causal inference. Practically every matching/weighting method traces back here.
- **[Cox (1975), *Partial likelihood*](https://doi.org/10.1093/biomet/62.2.269).** The technical justification for the Cox proportional hazards model — one of the most-cited papers in biostatistics history.
- **[Liang & Zeger (1986), *Longitudinal data analysis using generalized linear models*](https://doi.org/10.1093/biomet/73.1.13).** Introduced GEE for correlated data — the default tool for marginal longitudinal analysis in clinical and epidemiologic studies.

```{=html}
<div class="callout-note-custom">
<strong>Note on S-tier strategy:</strong> For a PhD student, targeting S-tier on a single-author first paper is ambitious and often unwise. More typical trajectories: publish your first methods paper in an A-tier venue with your advisor, then aim S-tier for work that builds on that foundation. Some advisors in our field prefer the opposite — aim high, absorb the rejection, resubmit downstream. Talk to yours.
</div>
```

:::


---

::: {.tier-a}

# A tier — core homes for semiparametric and causal work {#a-tier}

These journals are where most of the methodological work in our subfield actually lives. Expect to place many of your career papers here.

## [Biometrics](https://academic.oup.com/biometrics) {#biometrics}

**What it is:** The flagship journal of the International Biometric Society, published by Oxford University Press. The natural home for biostatistical methodology.

**Focus:** Methodology with clear biological or medical applications — survival analysis, longitudinal data, clinical trials, causal inference in health settings, and genomics methodology.

**When to target:** The bar is high but the audience is explicitly biostatistics rather than general statistics, so a paper well-suited to *Biometrics* might be out of scope at JRSS-B. This is a default first choice for methods papers with a clinical or health-data motivation.

**Representative papers:**

- **[Zhang, Tsiatis & Davidian (2008), *Improving efficiency of inferences in randomized clinical trials using auxiliary covariates*](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1541-0420.2007.00976.x).** Semiparametric-efficient covariate adjustment for RCTs — anticipated today's FDA guidance on covariate adjustment in trial analyses.

## [Biostatistics](https://academic.oup.com/biostatistics) {#biostatistics}

**What it is:** The Oxford-published biostatistics journal. Somewhat more theoretical in flavor than *Biometrics*, with a smaller page count.

**Focus:** Methods for biomedical applications with an emphasis on genomics, imaging, and complex observational data. Welcoming to semiparametric and causal methodology.

**When to target:** A strong fit when the methodological contribution leans toward high-dimensional or genomics-flavored biomedical problems. If the paper is more clinical-trial or survival-oriented, *Biometrics* is usually the better first stop.

## [JASA Applications & Case Studies](https://www.tandfonline.com/journals/uasa20) {#jasa-acs}

**What it is:** The Applications & Case Studies (A&CS) section of JASA, published by Taylor & Francis for the American Statistical Association. Different editorial track from the Theory & Methods section.

**Focus:** Applied statistics with methodological contribution, or methodology developed in service of a substantive application. Papers typically tell a strong data story.

**When to target:** If the paper's primary contribution is the substantive answer rather than the method, A&CS is the better fit; if the method itself is the contribution, JASA T&M is the natural home. A&CS lands here in A tier because the methodological content it demands is genuinely rigorous.

## [Epidemiology](https://journals.lww.com/epidem/pages/default.aspx) {#epidemiology}

**What it is:** The flagship methodological epidemiology journal, published by Wolters Kluwer (Lippincott). Short paper format.

**Focus:** Methodological and conceptual papers in epidemiology, with a heavy emphasis on causal inference.

**When to target:** A remarkable amount of the *conceptual* foundation of modern causal inference lives here — Hernán, Robins, VanderWeele, Greenland and others publish foundational work in *Epidemiology* that then gets formalized elsewhere. The implicit editorial signal is *"a clean conceptual contribution or an identification argument that clarifies something"*, so it is the right home for short, idea-dense papers; a heavier formal-theorem paper usually belongs at *Biometrics* or a stats venue instead.

**Representative papers:**

- **[Hernán, Brumback & Robins (2000), *Marginal structural models to estimate the causal effect of zidovudine on the survival of HIV-positive men*](https://doi.org/10.1097/00001648-200009000-00012).** The paper that brought MSMs and IPTW from theory to practice; the template for essentially every time-varying confounding analysis done since.
- **[Greenland, Pearl & Robins (1999), *Causal diagrams for epidemiologic research*](https://doi.org/10.1097/00001648-199901000-00008).** Introduced DAGs to epidemiologic methodology and fixed a generation of vocabulary around confounding, colliders, and mediators.
- **[VanderWeele (2014), *A unification of mediation and interaction: a four-way decomposition*](https://doi.org/10.1097/EDE.0000000000000121).** Clarified how mediation and interaction combine into a single additive decomposition — now the standard framework for mediation analyses in health research.

## [Journal of Econometrics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/journal-of-econometrics) {#j-econometrics}

**What it is:** The leading econometrics journal, published by Elsevier.

**Focus:** Econometric theory and methodology. Strong on semiparametric efficiency, instrumental variables, moment-based estimation, and debiased ML.

**When to target:** The econometrics literature has developed a parallel and often more technically developed treatment of semiparametric inference, so if your paper has an IV or policy-evaluation flavor this can be the best audience — econometricians will cite it. Prefer it over *Biometrics* when the identification story is economic rather than biomedical.

**Representative papers:**

- **[Abadie (2003), *Semiparametric instrumental variable estimation of treatment response models*](https://doi.org/10.1016/S0304-4076(02)00201-4).** A cornerstone paper for estimating local average treatment effects when the instrument satisfies LATE-type assumptions.
- **[Frölich (2007), *Nonparametric IV estimation of local average treatment effects with covariates*](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jeconom.2006.07.001).** Extended LATE estimation to settings with high-dimensional covariates — practically relevant for modern program evaluation.

## [Journal of Machine Learning Research](https://www.jmlr.org/) {#jmlr}

**What it is:** Open-access flagship machine learning journal. Peer-reviewed and longer-form than conference papers.

**Focus:** Machine learning theory and methodology, including a growing body of causal ML work.

**When to target:** Much of the modern causal-ML literature (heterogeneous treatment effects, policy learning, conformal prediction for causal quantities) lives here, and editorial standards are genuinely high — it is the natural venue for the journal version of conference-first causal-ML work. Caveat: some classical statistics departments still discount ML venues for tenure, though this has shifted substantially in the last decade.

## [Econometrica](https://www.econometricsociety.org/publications/econometrica) {#econometrica}

**What it is:** The top general econometrics/economics theory journal, published by the Econometric Society.

**Focus:** Foundational theoretical contributions in econometrics and economic theory.

**When to target:** Landing here is extremely hard and a major signal, but the readership is economists and papers are read through that lens. For a biostatistician it is a reach venue rather than a natural home — appropriate when the contribution is a foundational identification or efficiency result with clear economic framing.

**Representative papers:**

- **[Imbens & Angrist (1994), *Identification and estimation of local average treatment effects*](https://doi.org/10.2307/2951620).** The LATE paper — introduced the compliance-type framework that underlies modern IV analysis.
- **[Hahn (1998), *On the role of the propensity score in efficient semiparametric estimation of average treatment effects*](https://doi.org/10.2307/2998560).** Derived the semiparametric efficiency bound for the ATE — the theoretical foundation that every efficient ATE estimator targets.
- **[Hirano, Imbens & Ridder (2003), *Efficient estimation of average treatment effects using the estimated propensity score*](https://doi.org/10.1111/1468-0262.00442).** Showed that nonparametrically-estimated propensity scores yield efficient ATE estimators — a result that motivates much of the "estimate the nuisance, plug in" approach to causal inference.

## [International Journal of Biostatistics](https://www.degruyterbrill.com/journal/key/ijb/html) {#ijb}

**What it is:** De Gruyter-published biostatistics journal with strong ties to the targeted learning community.

**Focus:** Biostatistics with an emphasis on semiparametric methods, targeted learning, and causal inference. More methodological than clinical-trial focused.

**When to target:** Editorial lineage traces directly through the targeted learning community, and a very high fraction of the TMLE methodology literature has landed here — a natural fit for targeted-learning work. Caveat: its impact factor is modest, which matters for external evaluators, though within the subfield it is a respected venue.

**Representative papers:**

- **[van der Laan & Rubin (2006), *Targeted maximum likelihood learning*](https://doi.org/10.2202/1557-4679.1043).** The paper that introduced TMLE — the canonical reference for the entire framework.
- **[Gruber & van der Laan (2010), *A targeted maximum likelihood estimator of a causal effect on a bounded continuous outcome*](https://doi.org/10.2202/1557-4679.1260).** Extended TMLE to bounded continuous outcomes via a logistic fluctuation — widely used in applied TMLE analyses.

## [Journal of Causal Inference](https://www.degruyterbrill.com/journal/key/jci/html) {#j-causal-inference}

**What it is:** De Gruyter-published journal dedicated to causal inference methodology.

**Focus:** Causal inference across fields — statistics, epidemiology, economics, and computer science. Publishes work that might sit awkwardly between disciplinary homes.

**When to target:** The right venue for methodology that is genuinely cross-disciplinary or graphical/identification-flavored and would not find a natural reviewer pool at *Biometrics*, *Journal of Econometrics*, or *Epidemiology*.

:::


---

::: {.tier-b}

# B tier — strong and relevant, often audience-specific {#b-tier}

## [Annals of Applied Statistics](https://imstat.org/journals-and-publications/annals-of-applied-statistics/) {#aoas}

**What it is:** The applied counterpart to *Annals of Statistics*, published by IMS.

**Focus:** Substantive applied statistics with methodological contribution. Papers here typically tell the story of an interesting real problem and the method developed for it.

**When to target:** Aim here when you have a serious applied dataset driving a genuine methodological advance. AoAS is genuinely excellent but sometimes undervalued on CVs because "Annals of Applied" sounds less prestigious than "Annals of" — within the field it carries significant weight.

## [American Journal of Epidemiology](https://academic.oup.com/aje) {#aje}

**What it is:** The other flagship epidemiology journal (alongside *Epidemiology*), published by Oxford University Press for the Society for Epidemiologic Research.

**Focus:** Broader than *Epidemiology* — publishes applied epidemiology alongside methodology. For methodological papers, placement is similar.

**When to target:** Strong fit for causal-inference and epidemiologic methods papers that want a broad applied-epi readership. Lands here rather than in A tier because the methodological bar and audience skew more applied than *Epidemiology*.

**Representative papers:**

- **[Hernán & Robins (2016), *Using big data to emulate a target trial when a randomized trial is not available*](https://doi.org/10.1093/aje/kwv254).** The target-trial framework paper — now the dominant template for observational comparative-effectiveness analyses.
- **[VanderWeele & Vansteelandt (2010), *Odds ratios for mediation analysis for a dichotomous outcome*](https://doi.org/10.1093/aje/kwq332).** Practical mediation analysis for binary outcomes; widely cited in applied epi.

## [Statistical Science](https://imstat.org/journals-and-publications/statistical-science/) {#statistical-science}

**What it is:** IMS-published review and perspectives journal.

**Focus:** Review articles, methodological perspectives, interviews with prominent statisticians. Papers are typically invited or heavily shepherded.

**When to target:** Not a standard submission venue — most placement comes via invitation or strong editorial shepherding. It lands in B rather than higher because you cannot reliably target it, but the papers it publishes are often agenda-setting and worth reading before starting a new research direction.

**Representative papers:**

- **[Breiman (2001), *Statistical modeling: the two cultures*](https://doi.org/10.1214/ss/1009213726).** The essay that named and framed the "data modeling" vs. "algorithmic modeling" split — still a reference point in every discussion of stats vs. ML culture.
- **[Kang & Schafer (2007), *Demystifying double robustness: a comparison of alternative strategies for estimating a population mean from incomplete data*](https://doi.org/10.1214/07-STS227).** A clarifying review of DR estimation's practical behavior that shaped how the field teaches and uses these methods.

## [Statistics in Medicine](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/10970258) {#stats-in-medicine}

**What it is:** Wiley-published applied biostatistics journal. High volume, high impact factor.

**Focus:** Methodology and applications for medical research. Clinical trials methodology, survival analysis, longitudinal data, epidemiological methods.

**When to target:** Natural home for applied biostatistics methodology with a clear clinical motivation. Tension to weigh: higher IF than most A-tier journals but lower methodological prestige — it publishes many useful papers but also more papers of lower novelty than A-tier venues. Prefer *Biometrics* or *Biostatistics* when the methodological novelty is the core contribution.

**Representative papers:**

- **[Austin (2009), *Balance diagnostics for comparing the distribution of baseline covariates between treatment groups in propensity-score matched samples*](https://doi.org/10.1002/sim.3697).** The standard applied-statistics reference for how to report balance in a PS-matched analysis.
- **[Royston & Parmar (2002), *Flexible parametric proportional-hazards and proportional-odds models for censored survival data, with application to prognostic modelling and estimation of treatment effects*](https://doi.org/10.1002/sim.1203).** Introduced the flexible-parametric spline-based survival framework widely used in clinical research today.

## [Bernoulli](https://projecteuclid.org/journals/bernoulli) {#bernoulli}

**What it is:** Journal of the Bernoulli Society for Mathematical Statistics and Probability.

**Focus:** Mathematical statistics and probability. Strong on empirical process theory, high-dimensional statistics, and theoretical aspects of learning theory.

**When to target:** Deeply theoretical papers that are probabilistic in flavor and may not fit AoS or JRSS-B's scope. Choose Bernoulli over EJS when the probabilistic machinery itself is the contribution.

## [JRSS-A (Statistics in Society)](https://academic.oup.com/jrsssa) {#jrss-a}

**What it is:** The Royal Statistical Society's applied journal, with a policy/social-science focus.

**Focus:** Applied statistics for public policy, social science, and official statistics — work where methodological substance meets policy implications.

**When to target:** Good home for policy-evaluation work with a public-policy audience, especially when the paper has both methodological substance and policy implications. Pick JRSS-A over REStat when the audience you want is statisticians and policy analysts rather than economists.

## [Review of Economics and Statistics](https://direct.mit.edu/rest) {#restat}

**What it is:** MIT Press–published applied economics journal at the intersection of econometrics and economics.

**Focus:** Empirical economics with a strong program-evaluation and applied-econometrics emphasis.

**When to target:** If your work has a program-evaluation flavor that reaches economists (labor economics, health economics), this can be a better audience than a pure stats venue. Prefer REStat over JRSS-A when the substantive question and framing will be read by economists.

**Representative papers:**

- **[Dehejia & Wahba (2002), *Propensity score-matching methods for nonexperimental causal studies*](https://doi.org/10.1162/003465302317331982).** The paper that popularized PS matching in applied economics and triggered the long LaLonde-replication debate.

## [Observational Studies](https://muse.jhu.edu/journal/733) {#observational-studies}

**What it is:** Dedicated journal for observational research methodology.

**Focus:** Methodology for observational data, including sensitivity analysis, matching, causal inference in complex settings.

**When to target:** Sensitivity-analysis and matched-design methodology fits naturally here. It lands in B despite modest size because of its strong editorial board, methodological rigor, and niche specificity — the tradeoff is narrower readership than broader causal venues.

## [Electronic Journal of Statistics](https://imstat.org/journals-and-publications/electronic-journal-of-statistics/) {#ejs}

**What it is:** Open-access IMS journal.

**Focus:** Theoretical and methodological statistics, similar in scope to AoS/JRSS-B but less selective. Fast turnaround by stats-journal standards.

**When to target:** Good option when you want a rigorous theoretical paper out quickly and open-access. Prefer EJS over Bernoulli when the contribution is statistical methodology rather than probabilistic machinery.

## [Pharmaceutical Statistics](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15391612) {#pharm-stats}

**What it is:** Wiley-published journal of the pharmaceutical industry biostatistics community.

**Focus:** Clinical trials methodology, adaptive designs, regulatory statistics, external control arms, estimands (ICH E9 R1), real-world evidence for regulatory submissions.

**When to target:** Papers that engage directly with pharma/regulatory methodology. Pick this over *Statistics in Medicine* when the audience you most want to reach is industry biostatisticians and regulators.

:::


---

::: {.tier-c}

# C tier — respectable niche and methods-applied outlets {#c-tier}

These are legitimate venues — none should be confused with predatory or low-quality journals. They are here because they are either very specialized, oriented toward application over methodology, or have looser editorial standards than A/B tier.

## [Statistical Methods in Medical Research](https://journals.sagepub.com/home/smm) {#smmr}

**What it is:** Sage-published applied biostatistics methodology journal. A long-running outlet for medical-statistics methods aimed at a biomedical readership.

**Focus:** Methodology for medical research, including survival analysis, longitudinal data, causal inference in health settings, meta-analysis, and clinical-trial methods. Tutorial and review papers feature prominently.

**When to target:** Good home for methods-oriented biomedical papers that are solid but not quite competitive at *Biometrics* or *Statistics in Medicine*. Quality is variable; often a second-choice venue for papers not placed at those flagships.

## [Journal of Clinical Epidemiology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/journal-of-clinical-epidemiology) {#j-clin-epi}

**What it is:** Elsevier-published clinical-epidemiology journal; the official journal of several evidence-based-medicine groups, with a heavy reporting-and-guidelines orientation.

**Focus:** Methodology for conducting, reporting, and synthesizing clinical research. Prediction models, systematic reviews, and reporting guidelines dominate.

**When to target:** Natural home for papers on how clinical evidence should be produced and reported, rather than frontier methodology. Editorial appetite is strongest for work with direct implications for clinical-research practice.

## [Health Services Research](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/14756773) {#hsr}

**What it is:** Wiley-published journal of AcademyHealth, the professional society for health-services research. The field's flagship journal.

**Focus:** Empirical health-services research — access, utilization, cost, quality, and disparities — with quantitative methods applied to administrative, claims, and survey data.

**When to target:** Best fit when the primary contribution is a substantive health-services finding supported by careful identification or measurement. Audience is primarily health-services researchers rather than methodologists, so methodological novelty alone is not enough.

## [Journal of Health Economics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/journal-of-health-economics) {#j-health-econ}

**What it is:** Elsevier-published field journal for health economics. A leading outlet for empirical and theoretical work on health and healthcare markets.

**Focus:** Health-economics theory and empirics, with strong econometric engine rooms — program evaluation, insurance-market analysis, health behavior, and provider incentives.

**When to target:** Right audience when the paper has a serious econometric identification strategy and a health-economics substantive question. Methodology-only papers fit poorly; the editorial bar is on economic content as much as on statistical technique.

## [Lifetime Data Analysis](https://link.springer.com/journal/10985) {#lifetime-data-analysis}

**What it is:** Springer-published specialty journal for survival and time-to-event analysis. A focused technical outlet for the survival-methodology community.

**Focus:** Statistical methodology for lifetime data — censored, truncated, recurrent, multi-state, and competing-risks processes — with emphasis on semiparametric and likelihood-based approaches.

**When to target:** Natural home for genuinely survival-specific methodology that would be treated as narrow at a general biostatistics journal. Readership is small but expert, so technical precision is valued.

## [Scandinavian Journal of Statistics](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/14679469) {#scand-j-stat}

**What it is:** Wiley-published journal of the Nordic statistics societies. A general mathematical-statistics journal with a long European tradition.

**Focus:** General statistical methodology and theory, with some Nordic flavor in empirical-process, likelihood-based, and semiparametric traditions. Open to both theory and methodology with biomedical or applied motivation.

**When to target:** Reasonable venue for rigorous methodology papers that need a theoretically-minded audience but are unlikely to land at Biometrika or JRSS-B. Similar scope to EJS but with lower visibility.

## [Econometric Theory](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/econometric-theory) {#econometric-theory}

**What it is:** Cambridge-published theoretical econometrics journal. A specialized outlet for the mathematical underpinnings of econometric methods.

**Focus:** Asymptotic theory, weak-regularity results, higher-order expansions, and bootstrap theory for econometric models. Narrower and more technical than *Journal of Econometrics*.

**When to target:** Consider when the contribution is a technical asymptotic result rather than a new estimator or application. Audience is theoretical econometricians; biostatistics readers are largely absent.

## [Computational Statistics and Data Analysis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/computational-statistics-and-data-analysis) {#csda}

**What it is:** Elsevier-published journal focused on computational statistics and algorithms. A principal outlet for the computational-statistics community.

**Focus:** Computationally-oriented statistical methodology — simulation, optimization, sampling, and scalable inference — with an appetite for algorithmic contributions alongside traditional methodology.

**When to target:** Good match when the novelty is primarily computational — a new algorithm, a scalability improvement, or a careful empirical evaluation of existing methods. Less suitable for theorem-driven papers without a computational hook.

## [Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series C (Applied Statistics)](https://academic.oup.com/jrsssc) {#jrss-c}

**What it is:** The Royal Statistical Society's applied-statistics journal (now published on Oxford University Press). The applied counterpart within the JRSS family.

**Focus:** Applied statistics across domains — environmental, demographic, biomedical, industrial, sports — where the motivating dataset drives the methodological choice.

**When to target:** Fit for papers where the interest is an applied case study with a modest methodological contribution. Content is heterogeneous in quality; signal is stronger when the application is novel and the method is well-matched.

## [Biometrical Journal](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15214036) {#biometrical-journal}

**What it is:** Wiley-published general biostatistics journal with a European / German-biometry lineage. A long-standing outlet for biometric methodology.

**Focus:** Biostatistical methodology, particularly clinical trials, biomarker analysis, longitudinal data, and survival modeling in biomedical contexts.

**When to target:** Reasonable venue for solid biostatistics methodology that does not reach the bar for *Biometrics* or *Biostatistics*. Respectable but lower-visibility than those flagships.

:::
